title: Power, Politics & Conflict
simple_html:
Power, politics, and conflict are the realities of organizational life that textbooks once treated as taboo. Power is the capacity to influence others. Politics is using power to get what you want, especially when resources are scarce. Conflict is what happens when interests, goals, or perceptions clash. Handled well, all three can be productive; handled badly, they are destructive.

Where power comes from

French and Raven identified five sources, split into two groups:

  • Positional power (from your role): legitimate (formal authority), reward (you control benefits), and coercive (you control punishment).
  • Personal power (from you): expert (knowledge/skill) and referent (others admire and identify with you).

Personal power generally produces better commitment and performance; coercive power breeds resentment.

Politics and influence

Organizational politics arise when resources are limited and goals are unclear. People use influence tactics — rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation, ingratiation, and so on. Some, like rational persuasion, are constructive; others, like pure pressure, are not.

Conflict can be good

Modern thinking sees conflict as natural and sometimes helpful. Task conflict (about ideas) at moderate levels improves decisions; relationship conflict (personal friction) almost always hurts. The goal isn't zero conflict — it's the right kind at the right level.

Key takeaways

  • Power has five bases; personal power (expert, referent) usually beats positional power.
  • Politics is neutral — a tool that can be used well or badly.
  • Some task conflict is healthy; relationship conflict is not.
  • Influence tactics vary in effectiveness; rational persuasion travels in all directions.
  • Conflict is managed through different handling styles, not eliminated.
intermediate_html:
Power, politics, and conflict are intertwined facets of how interests are pursued and reconciled in organizations. Power is the capacity of A to influence B to act against B's own preferences; politics is power in action — activities to acquire, develop, and use power to obtain preferred outcomes amid uncertainty and disagreement; conflict is the process that begins when one party perceives another has negatively affected something it cares about. This lesson covers the bases of power, dependency, influence tactics, the politics of organizations, and the modern view of conflict and negotiation.

Bases of power (French & Raven)

BaseSourceTypical effect
LegitimateFormal position/authorityCompliance
RewardControl over valued benefitsCompliance
CoerciveControl over punishmentCompliance + resentment
ExpertKnowledge and skillCommitment
ReferentAdmiration, identification, charismaCommitment

The first three are positional; the last two are personal. Research consistently shows personal power (expert and referent) produces commitment and performance, whereas coercive power produces resistance.

The dependency principle

Power flows from dependence: the more B depends on A, the more power A holds. Dependency rises when a resource is important, scarce, and non-substitutable. This explains why controlling a critical, rare, irreplaceable resource (a key client relationship, a unique technical skill) confers power regardless of formal title — the foundation of the strategic-contingencies view of intra-organizational power.

Influence tactics

Yukl's research identifies tactics including rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation, ingratiation, personal appeals, exchange, coalition, pressure, and legitimating. The most effective (producing commitment) are rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, and consultation; the least effective are pressure, coalition, and legitimating. Direction matters: rational persuasion works upward, downward, and laterally, while inspirational appeals and pressure work best downward.

Organizational politics

Politics intensifies when resources are scarce, goals and decision criteria are ambiguous, and performance is hard to measure objectively. Impression management — controlling how others perceive us through self-promotion, ingratiation, exemplification, and so on — is a key political behavior, valuable in interviews and performance reviews but risky if overdone. Politics is ethically neutral: the test is whether the behavior serves legitimate organizational interests or purely self-interested ones at others' expense.

The modern view of conflict

The interactionist view replaced the older "all conflict is bad" stance. It distinguishes functional conflict (constructive, supports goals) from dysfunctional conflict (destructive). The crucial type distinction:

  • Task conflict — disagreement about content and goals. At low-to-moderate levels it improves decision quality by surfacing alternatives and curbing groupthink.
  • Relationship conflict — interpersonal incompatibility and friction. Almost always dysfunctional; it drains energy and erodes trust.
  • Process conflict — disagreement about how work gets done; low levels can help, high levels hurt.

Conflict-handling styles (Thomas-Kilmann)

Two dimensions — assertiveness (concern for self) and cooperativeness (concern for others) — yield five styles: competing (win-lose), collaborating (win-win), compromising (split the difference), avoiding (withdraw), and accommodating (yield). No style is universally best; collaborating suits complex, important issues with time to invest, while competing suits emergencies needing decisive action.

Negotiation

Two approaches: distributive bargaining (fixed-pie, win-lose, e.g., haggling over price) and integrative bargaining (expand the pie, win-win, by trading across differently-valued issues). Key concepts: BATNA (Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement) sets your walk-away point and is your single greatest source of negotiating power.

Worked mini-case

Two department heads clash over a shared budget. If they treat it as distributive (fixed pie), one wins and resentment festers (relationship conflict). A skilled mediator reframes it as integrative: by uncovering that one head most values headcount while the other most values capital equipment, they trade across issues so each gets what it values most — a collaborating style applied to task conflict. Power dynamics matter too: the head with the stronger BATNA (an alternative funding source) negotiates from greater strength.

How to apply it

Build personal power (expertise, relationships) rather than relying on position; control important, scarce, non-substitutable resources; choose influence tactics that fit the direction and the person; encourage moderate task conflict while suppressing relationship conflict; match your conflict-handling style to the stakes and time available; and always know your BATNA before negotiating.
full_html:
Power, politics, and conflict form the "dark matter" of organizational behavior — pervasive, consequential, and long under-examined because they sit uneasily beside rational, harmonious images of organizations. This lesson develops a rigorous treatment: the conceptual relationship among the three, the bases of power and the dependency theory that underpins them, influence tactics and political behavior, the interactionist reframing of conflict, conflict-handling and negotiation frameworks, and the ethical questions that run throughout.

Defining the trio

Following Robbins and Judge, power is a capacity A possesses to influence the behavior of B so B acts in accordance with A's wishes; it is a function of dependence and need not be exercised to exist. Politics is power in action — Pfeffer (1981) defines organizational politics as activities to acquire, develop, and use power and other resources to obtain preferred outcomes where there is uncertainty or dissensus about choices. Conflict, per Thomas (1992), is a process beginning when one party perceives another has negatively affected, or is about to, something the first cares about. The three are sequential and recursive: scarce resources generate conflict; parties mobilize power and engage in politics to prevail; outcomes redistribute power and seed future conflict.

Bases of power and the dependency postulate

French and Raven's (1959) five bases — legitimate, reward, coercive, expert, referent (later extended with informational power) — split into positional (legitimate, reward, coercive) and personal (expert, referent). Empirical syntheses show personal power yields commitment and is positively associated with performance and satisfaction, whereas coercive power yields resistance and dissatisfaction; reward and legitimate power yield mere compliance. Underlying all bases is Emerson's (1962) dependency principle: P(A over B) is proportional to B's dependence on A, which rises with the resource's importance, scarcity, and non-substitutability. The strategic contingencies theory (Hickson et al., 1971) extends this to subunits: departments accrue power by coping with critical uncertainties, being central to workflow, and being non-substitutable — which is why, historically, finance or engineering may dominate depending on the firm's key contingency. Resource dependence theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 1978) scales the logic to inter-organizational relations.

Influence tactics

Where power is the capacity, influence tactics are its enactment. Yukl and colleagues catalog: rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation, ingratiation, personal appeals, exchange, coalition tactics, legitimating, and pressure. Effectiveness research distinguishes outcomes — commitment > compliance > resistance. The "core" tactics (rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation) most often produce commitment; "hard" tactics (pressure, legitimating, coalition) tend toward compliance or resistance. Tactic effectiveness is directional: rational persuasion is the only tactic that travels effectively upward, downward, and laterally; inspirational appeals and pressure are downward tactics; personal appeals and exchange work best laterally. Sequencing and combining "soft" before "hard" tactics improves outcomes.

Organizational politics and impression management

Politics flourishes under specific conditions (Pfeffer): resource scarcity, goal ambiguity, non-routine technology, and zero-sum decisions. Individual antecedents include high need for power, Machiavellianism, and an internal locus of control; perceptions of politics generally correlate with lower satisfaction and higher turnover intentions, though the relationship is moderated by individuals' political skill. Impression management (Goffman, 1959; Leary & Kowalski, 1990) — the process of controlling others' perceptions through tactics such as self-promotion, ingratiation, exemplification, intimidation, and supplication — is a central political competence, demonstrably affecting interview and appraisal outcomes, but it carries reputational risk when perceived as inauthentic. The ethical frame (Cavanagh et al., 1981) evaluates political behavior on utilitarian outcomes, rights, and justice: behavior serving genuine organizational ends within these bounds is legitimate; self-serving manipulation at others' expense is not.

The interactionist view of conflict

Conflict thought evolved through three views: the traditional (all conflict is bad and avoidable), the human relations (conflict is natural and inevitable), and the contemporary interactionist (a minimum level of conflict is necessary for a group to remain viable and creative). The interactionist view's pivotal contribution is the functional vs. dysfunctional distinction, refined by type: De Dreu and Weingart's (2003) meta-analysis confirmed that relationship conflict is robustly dysfunctional (harming performance and satisfaction), while task conflict shows a more complex, often inverted-U relationship — moderate task conflict can enhance decision quality in non-routine tasks by countering groupthink (Janis, 1972; see Lesson 5), but high task conflict tends to spill into relationship conflict. Process conflict (over delegation and resource allocation) is generally harmful above low levels. The practical implication is paradoxical: managers should sometimes stimulate functional task conflict (devil's advocacy, dialectical inquiry, diverse staffing) while vigilantly suppressing relationship conflict.

Conflict-handling and negotiation frameworks

The Thomas-Kilmann (1974) dual-concern model crosses assertiveness (concern for self) with cooperativeness (concern for others) to yield five styles: competing (high/low), collaborating (high/high), compromising (mid/mid), avoiding (low/low), and accommodating (low/high). Each is appropriate under different conditions — collaborating for high-stakes, integrative problems with time available; competing for emergencies and unpopular-but-necessary actions; avoiding for trivial issues or to let tempers cool. Negotiation is the applied resolution process. Distributive bargaining divides a fixed pie (win-lose, claiming value), governed by each side's resistance point and the resulting bargaining zone (ZOPA). Integrative bargaining (Walton & McKersie, 1965; Fisher & Ury's Getting to Yes, 1981) expands the pie by trading across issues valued differently, separating people from the problem, focusing on interests not positions, and inventing options for mutual gain. The single most important leverage concept is the BATNA (Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement) — the stronger your BATNA, the more power you wield and the higher your reservation point.

Worked example

Two VPs contest a fixed innovation budget. Framed distributively, the bargaining zone is narrow and the loser exits resentful (relationship conflict, lower future cooperation). A facilitator applies an integrative approach: probing interests reveals VP-A urgently needs talent (headcount) while VP-B needs capital (equipment) with a longer horizon. By logrolling — A concedes on equipment timing, B concedes on hiring — both exceed what a 50/50 split would yield. Power analysis: A has a credible external-grant BATNA, strengthening A's reservation point; B counters by building a coalition with the CFO (a hard tactic) and using rational persuasion on ROI (a core tactic). The case integrates dependency, influence tactics, conflict type, handling style, and negotiation in one episode.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating power with formal authority — overlooking expert/referent (personal) power and dependency.
  • Believing all conflict is harmful — suppressing functional task conflict and inviting groupthink.
  • Treating politics as inherently unethical — it is a neutral tool judged by ends and means.
  • Defaulting to distributive bargaining when integrative gains are available (the "fixed-pie" bias).
  • Entering negotiation without identifying one's BATNA — the most common and costly error.
  • Over-relying on coercive power or hard influence tactics, generating compliance-with-resentment rather than commitment.

Cross-links

This lesson operationalizes Leadership (Lesson 7 — bases of power are the leader's currency; referent power underlies charisma), is enacted through Communication (Lesson 6 — influence tactics and negotiation are applied communication), connects to Groups & Teams (Lesson 5 — functional task conflict counters groupthink; process conflict is a team pathology), draws on Individual Differences (Lesson 2 — Machiavellianism, locus of control, and the Dark Triad shape political behavior), and feeds Organizational Culture & Change (Lesson 9 — resistance to change is often a political and power phenomenon).
